nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, clear tropical reefs over coral or rocky bottoms; perches and hops among rockwork.

diet.notes: Grazes on microalgae across rockwork. Diet should be supplemented with marine algae, nori, spirulina, and algae-based prepared foods 2–3 times daily, particularly if natural algae growth is limited.

goodWith: hardy reef fish that do not resemble blennies

imageCandidates.notes: NOAA Photo Library, 1456x786. Sharp in-situ shot of a live Red Lip Blenny peering from a ledge against a red sponge; bright red lips and yellow eye-ring clearly shown against a vivid reef backdrop. Most striking of the set.
Nick Hobgood (Nhobgood), 1800x2400. Crisp full-body dorsal-lateral view showing the dark brown body, red lips and bright orange pelvic fins; whole animal in frame on live rock.

q phia, 4519x2824. Tack-sharp high-res head-and-shoulders portrait (Cuba) showing the diagnostic blunt horse-like head, cirri and large eye; body partly out of frame so kept as a non-recommended detail shot.

summary: Caribbean reef blenny with bright red lips and a blunt, horse-like head. Perches and hops over rockwork grazing on algae; generally territorial and best kept singly.
careNotes: The Red Lip Blenny is generally considered an algae-grazing utility fish for established reef tanks with substantial live rock. It tends to be territorial — particularly toward other blennies, gobies, and dartfish — and is best kept as the only blenny in the system unless housed in a larger tank. Has been reported to nip at small-polyped stony corals (SPS corals, where many tiny polyps cover a hard calcium carbonate skeleton) and clam mantles, so its reef-safety is generally described as 'with caution' rather than fully reef-safe. Like most blennies, the Red Lip Blenny has been known to jump from open tanks, and a tight-fitting lid is generally advised. A minimum of around 200 L (~53 US gal) with at least 100 cm of tank length is recommended, reflecting the species' active hopping and perching behavior, high conspecific aggression, and adult size of around 12 cm. The name *Ophioblennius atlanticus* is still encountered in the aquarium trade, but Caribbean populations are now treated as a distinct species, *O. macclurei* (Silvester, 1915); *O. atlanticus* in the strict sense refers to the East Atlantic population.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing; eggs are demersal and adhesive, attached to a substrate. Captive breeding is not commonly reported.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Listed under the legacy name Ophioblennius atlanticus.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Assigned secondary returned HTTP 403; cross-verification fell through to FishBase, Quality Marine, and Fishkeeping World.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Ophioblennius macclurei, Silvester 1915), family Blenniidae, max length 12.2 cm TL, temperature 26.2–28.2°C wild, Caribbean/Western Central Atlantic range, territorial, oviparous with distinct pairing.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Recommends a 70 US gal minimum (~265 L) and describes the species as very aggressive/territorial toward smaller tankmates — the high end of the source spread.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Care summary aggregated via web search snippet (page itself 404'd): 10–13 cm adult length, 22–26°C, pH 8.0–8.5, SG 1.020–1.026, algae-grazing diet, jumping risk.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing; eggs demersal and adhesive. Captive breeding is uncommon and not well documented for the home aquarium.
